paper.title: Systematic benchmarking demonstrates large language models have not reached the diagnostic accuracy of traditional rare-disease decision support tools
paper.claim: Across 5,213 published rare-disease case reports (Phenopackets → HPO terms), the best LLM ranked the correct diagnosis first in 23.6% of cases, versus 35.5% for Exomiser, a traditional phenotype-driven tool. LLMs are improving but have not reached the accuracy of established bioinformatics tools.
paper.framing: Models keep improving, and this task is fully reproducible: the pipeline (phenopacket2prompt, MIT) and data are open. This tracks how the LLM-vs-Exomiser gap looks as newer models ship, on the paper's own task and Top-1 metric. It is not a verdict on the paper.
paper.readiness_caveat: Top-1 rank accuracy is one axis. The paper's own point stands regardless of newer models: an LLM matching Exomiser's Top-1 rate would still need calibration, abstention and transparent reasoning before clinical use.
paper.version_drift_note: Every LLM tested here is from 2023–2024 (o1-preview and o1-mini from Sep 2024, GPT-4o Aug 2024, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Meditron). That is the shelf-life gap StillTrueToday exists to close. The reasoning-model generation after o1 is not represented.
paper.status_note: Seeded with the paper's published Top-1 numbers (read from Fig 3; Exomiser 35.5% and best-LLM o1-preview 23.6% are exact from the text, other LLM bars are approximate). Reproduce-then-extend is feasible via the authors' open MIT pipeline + Zenodo data. That is the next build step for this paper.

claim_status.basis_pending: Frontier models are running now; the verdict resolves when they land.

auto.protocol_caveat: under StillTrueToday's LLM-judge, which grades a few points more leniently than the paper's programmatic grounding

contributors.name: Reese et al. (paper authors, Monarch Initiative)
contributors.role: Original benchmark of 5,213 phenopackets, the open-source phenopacket2prompt pipeline (MIT), and the published LLM-vs-Exomiser results.

protocols.label: Rare-disease diagnosis · 5,213 phenopackets · Top-1 rank
protocols.benchmark: Rare-disease differential diagnosis (5,213 published case reports)
protocols.dataset_slice: 5,213 phenopackets (HPO terms → ranked OMIM/Orphanet diagnosis), phenotype-only
protocols.scoring_method: programmatic match of ranked candidate to gold diagnosis via Mondo grounding (PhEval); correct diagnosis at rank 1
protocols.provenance: Top-1 values read from Fig 3. Exomiser (35.5%) and o1-preview (23.6%, the best LLM) are EXACT from the paper text; the other LLM bars are APPROXIMATE reads from the figure and flagged. Medfound-176B scored <=0.25% (effectively 0).

protocols.label: Rare-disease diagnosis · 50-case subset · Top-1 (StillTrueToday run)
protocols.benchmark: Rare-disease differential diagnosis (5,213 published case reports)
protocols.dataset_slice: first 50 cases of the paper's Zenodo corpus (record 15324355)
protocols.scoring_method: authors' exact prompts; rank of gold diagnosis in the model's ranked list found by an LLM judge (gpt-4o-2024-08-06), via OpenRouter
protocols.provenance: Numbers produced BY StillTrueToday (2026-07) on the paper's own Zenodo prompts. Our LLM-judge grading runs ~3–4 pts more lenient than the paper's PhEval programmatic grounding. Validated: we scored the authors' own stored o1-preview responses at 26.0% against their published 23.6%. So compare within this series, and treat absolute values against the 35.5% line with that bias in mind.

runs.notes: scored from authors' stored responses; validation vs published 23.6%
best current model; nominally at Exomiser's 35.5% but within our judge's leniency margin
returned a single diagnosis rather than a ranked list (Top-1=Top-3=Top-10), score is a format artifact, not a clean capability read
